segments.s776-g9: نباید که is an impersonal modal clause meaning 'one should not / it must not be that'; که introduces the subordinate clause. ماهوی سوری here functions as a nominal phrase (an epithet or name + ethnic adjective) 'the Syrian Māhūy'; ز جنگ attaches the cause/domain ('of/from battle') to بترسد in the next segment. Word order is standard Persian subordinate: modal + که + noun phrase + ز + noun.
segments.s776-g10: بترسد is present-subjunctive/defective-subjunctive meaning 'be afraid' as the verb of the clause introduced by نباید که. به + جیحون indicates direction ('to/toward the Jihun'), and کشد here means 'take/bring/drag' (an action affecting the person just mentioned); بی‌درنگ is an adverb 'immediately'. The verb phrase completes the command/advice begun in the previous segment.
segments.s776-g11: This is an imperative/advisory clause whose adverbial preface به تیزی means 'with haste' or 'quickly'. ازو ('from him') modifies چشم خود ('your own eye/gaze'); برمدار here is a compound verb-form meaning 'do not remove/turn aside' (literal components suggest 'keep/hold on'), so the clause means roughly 'do not hastily take your eyes away from him'. Word order places adverb of manner first for emphasis.
segments.s776-g12: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause 'for/since'; با او ('with him') marks the person involved; دگرگونه سازیم is present-subjunctive/hortative first-person-plural meaning 'we will make/do differently' (سازیم = 'we arrange/make'), and کار is the object 'the affair/plan'. The clause explains why you should not look away.
segments.s776-g13: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; برسام is the proper name subject; چینی modifies درفشش, 'his Chinese banner' (چینی = 'Chinese' as an adjective); بدید is the simple past 'saw'. The clause is a standard temporal subordinate: 'when X saw Y'.
segments.s776-g14: سپه را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker ('the army'); ز لشکر 'from the host' expresses separation or removal; به یکسو is a directional phrase 'to one side / to a flank'; کشید is the past tense verb 'drew / pulled / moved' completing the action. The line describes his tactical movement after seeing the banner.
segments.s776-g15: همی‌تاخت is an archaic/imperfective past form meaning 'he was charging / he kept charging'; تا + پیش gives the limit 'until before'; ریگ فرب is a noun phrase 'the sand of Farb' or literally 'fat/rich sand' (place/description). The clause sets the temporal/spatial extent of the charging action.
segments.s776-g16: This is a descriptive verbless (nominal) sequence typical of epic poetry: پرآژنگ رخ 'an ornamented/gaudily colored face' (پرآژنگ modifies رخ), پر ز دشنام لب 'lips full of curses' uses پر + ز to mean 'full of' and links back to the subject of the previous clause. The phrase piles vivid epithets rather than using a finite verb.
segments.s776-g33: This is a stacked nominal phrase: جهانجوی (a compound noun) is modified by the following adjectives ماهوی and شوریده‌هش. Adjectives follow the noun in this poetic register; شوریده‌هش is an archaic/poetic adjective meaning ‘distraught/frenzied’ describing the state of that world-seeking person.
segments.s776-g34: پرآزار و بی‌دین are two coordinate adjectives joined by و and further characterize the same referent; خداوندکش is a compound noun appended as an epithet meaning ‘killer of lords/sovereigns’. The whole string is a sequence of epithets (adjectival and nominal) piling up on the person introduced in the previous segment.
segments.s776-g35: چو marks a temporal clause ‘when’. The verb بشنید (past) appears before the subject بیژن (verb–subject order is common in older/epic Persian). از آن is a prepositional phrase ‘of/about that’ indicating what he heard; شاد شد is a change-of-state predicate ‘became happy’ with شد as the copular/change auxiliary.
segments.s776-g36: ببالید is a past-tense intransitive verb meaning ‘he exulted/grew proud’. و links the two verb phrases. ز اندیشه is a poetic prepositional phrase ‘from (or of) thought/worry’ and آزاد شد is ‘became free’ (شد as auxiliary) — together ‘was freed from thought/worry’.
segments.s776-g37: زدند is the past plural verb ‘they struck/struck out/made’. از بر ریگ نرم is a poetic locative/ablative phrase: از بر = ‘from/upon’, ریگ نرم = ‘soft sand’. شراعی is an archaic/poetic substantive placed before the verb; its precise meaning in this line is obscure (an archaic term used in epic diction) and does not match the supplied modern glosses exactly.
segments.s776-g38: همی‌رفت is the imperfective/past-continuous form ‘was going’ or ‘kept going’ in classical Persian. ماهوی is the subject (here ‘the spiritual/essential one’ carried over from the preceding description). چون introduces a simile ‘like’, and باد گرم is the simile’s vehicle ‘a warm wind’. The whole means ‘the ماهوی moved like a warm wind.’
segments.s776-g39: This is a temporal clause introduced by چون ‘when’. گنهکار is the subject ‘the sinner’ (person who has sinned), followed by the object phrase روی بیژن ‘the face of Bijan’ (Persian uses noun then possessor). بدید is the past-tense verb ‘saw’. The order here is subject – temporal marker – object – verb, which is acceptable in classical epic style.
segments.s776-g40: خرد شد literally ‘wisdom/intellect became’ — an idiom meaning his reason failed or was shattered (شد is the change-of-state auxiliary). ز مغز سرش is a genitive phrase ‘from the brain of his head’ and ناپدید is the predicate/adjective ‘vanished’. Together they describe the graphic result: his wits broke and the brain in his head disappeared.
segments.s776-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when' introducing a clause. The clause has subject بیژن (Bijan) then سپه را (the army, marked by را as definite/direct object) followed by همه راست کرد: راست کرد is a past-tense verbal phrase 'set straight / arranged' — here 'همه' modifies سپه (the whole army) so the sequence BIJAN — (the) ARMY-RA — WHOLE — arranged means 'Bijan arranged the whole army.'
segments.s776-g2: This is a second predicate linked to the first by the same subject (Bijan). به ایرانیان بر کمین is a prepositional phrase 'against / toward the Iranians in ambush' (به … بر کمین = an ambush directed at the Iranians) and خواست کرد is a past-tense periphrastic expression meaning 'desired / resolved' (خواست + کرد). Together: 'he resolved to lie in ambush for the Iranians.'
segments.s776-g3: بدانست is a past-tense verb 'he knew/perceived.' ماهوی here is an adjectival noun meaning 'essential / true in nature' (so 'he perceived the truth/essence'). و simply links the two actions. از قلبگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the heart / from the core' indicating the point of departure for the following verb.
segments.s776-g4: خروشان is an adverbial/participial adjective 'roaring / tumultuously' modifying the verb. برفت is the verbal element meaning 'went forth / rushed out' (an older compound past), but the listed senses in the data refer to 'snow' and do not match this verbal use (so no matching sense is available). از میان سپاه is the phrase 'out from the midst of the army' (از میان = out of/from the middle of; سپاه = army).
segments.s776-g5: نگه کرد is a compound past verb meaning 'kept watch / looked (attentively)'; the subject follows (بیژن). درفشش is 'his banner' (درفش + possessive suffix -ش). بدید is another past verb 'he saw.' The word order places the verbal action before the subject for poetic emphasis, then names the object and a second verb 'saw.'
segments.s776-g6: بدانست is again 'he knew / perceived.' کو here functions as 'where / which place.' جست is the past-stem 'to leap / spring'; خواهد is the auxiliary/future marker 'will'; گزید (in this context) is best read as 'choose / select' — together giving 'he knew where he would leap (i.e. which place he would choose to spring to).' Grammatically this is a noun-clause complement of 'بدانست.'
segments.s776-g7: به برسام فرمود is 'he said to Barsam' (به = to; فرمود = he commanded/said). کز قلبگاه is an adverbial phrase 'from the heart / from the center' where کز functions as 'from' (کز + noun expresses origin). Together this opens a direct command addressed to Barsam.
segments.s776-g8: به یکسو گذار is an imperative/instructional sequence meaning 'move/turn to one side' (به یکسو = to one side). گذار here is used as a verbal command 'place/move' though the supplied senses list gives only noun senses for گذار, so the precise verbal sense is not present in the provided options. آنک داری سپاه is a relative phrase 'that army which you have' (آنک = that/which; داری = you have; سپاه = army), specifying which troops to move.
segments.s776-g41: شد is the past/perfective of شدن 'to become' (here: 'he became') and appears before its complements as is common in epic/poetic word order. از بیم is an adverbial phrase 'out of/from fear' that licenses the resulting state; همچون + noun forms a simile ('like a ...'). تن is the noun 'body' and بی‌روان is an adjective 'without soul / lifeless', together 'like a lifeless body'.
segments.s776-g42: به سر بر is a prepositional cluster meaning 'onto/on the head' (به ... بر + noun); پراگند is a past-participial/adjectival form 'scattered' used predicatively, and ریگ روان is a noun phrase 'flowing sand' (روان here = 'flowing'). The sentence omits an explicit agent (they/it) and uses poetic word order where the scattered thing follows the locative phrase.
segments.s776-g43: بدو is the clitic/phrase 'to him' (indirect object before the verb), so بدو گفت بیژن = 'Bijan said to him'. که‌ای بدنژاد is an address: که‌ای (archaic/exclamatory 'O you') + بدنژاد 'low‑born / ignoble' functioning as a vocative ('O low‑born one').
segments.s776-g44: This is a negative optative clause introduced by که ('that/so that'): چون تو = 'such as you / like you', پرستار کس را = 'guardian/protector of anyone' with را marking the definite/definite‑like object, and مباد is an optative negative ('may it not be'). Word order places the optative verb (مباد) at the end, typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s776-g45: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. کشتی here is the past second‑person form of کشتن 'you killed' (note the homograph with the noun 'ship' — the provided noun senses don't include the verb, so the verb sense is inferred). آن دادگر is a determiner + epithet 'that just (one)' modifying شاه, and شاه را is the object marked by را; the implied subject of کشتی is 'you' (you killed...).
segments.s776-g46: This is an abbreviated optative/invocational clause: خداوند is 'God/the Lord' as a vocative or subject of an implied verb, پیروزی و گاه را = 'victory and time/long life' with را marking them as definite objects; a verb like 'grant' is omitted (elliptical), which is common in prayers/blessings in classical Persian.
segments.s776-g47: This noun phrase strings recipients/titles: پدر بر پدر شاه literally 'a father upon the father of the king' (poetic repetition for kinship/augmented sense) and و خود شهریار adds 'and to the king himself' (خود = 'self / himself' emphasizing the king). The phrase functions as a nominal unit in the larger predicate that follows.
segments.s776-g48: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' indicating source; نوشین‌روان is a descriptive epithet 'sweet‑souled' (agent/source of the memorial), در جهان is the locative 'in the world', and یادگار is the noun 'memorial / memento'. Word order is inverted compared with modern colloquial Persian (poetic placement of ز‑phrase before the locative and head noun).
segments.s776-g25: همانگه is an adverbial phrase 'at that moment'. رسیدند is the 3rd-person plural past tense 'they arrived/reached'. یاران اوی is the subject phrase ‘his companions’ — یاران (companions) plus the possessive enclitic اوی (= 'his/her') placed after the noun; word order here is somewhat inverted (adverb + verb + subject) for poetic effect.
segments.s776-g26: همه دشت is a collective subject 'the whole plain' (همه = entire, دشت = plain). شد is the past-tense copular/verb 'became'. پر از گفت‌وگوی is a predicate phrase 'full of conversation/about (him)' where پر 'full (of)' takes از + noun; ازو here indicates 'of/about him' (pointing back to the person the talk concerns). Note the noun گفت‌وگوی is in the construct form (written with the e/izāfa-like linking) meaning 'talk/conversation'.
segments.s776-g27: ببرسام is the addressee/name (Babr-sam). گفتند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they said/told' (here the sense list provided does not include the verbal sense 'said', so the proper sense is not listed). ک is a poetic/contracted form of که introducing the reported command/clause. این را (this + object-marking را) is the direct object 'this one', and مبر is the negative imperative 'do not take'. The verb گفتند precedes the indirect object in this order, which is common in narrative/poetry.
segments.s776-g28: بباید is a modal expression 'it is necessary / must' (present/past narrative sense). It is followed by the infinitive/masdar زدن 'to strike', and گردنش را is the direct object 'his neck' (گردن + possessive suffix ش = 'his' and object marker را). تبر (axe) appears after the clause and functions as the instrument (understood 'with the axe'); in poetic prose the instrumental may appear without an overt preposition.
segments.s776-g29: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. داد is the past tense of دادن 'gave', functioning here as a light verb with پاسخ to make 'gave an answer' (چنین داد پاسخ = 'he replied thus'). که introduces the content clause that follows. این راه نیست is the content clause 'this is not the way' (این = this, راه = way/method, نیست = is not).
segments.s776-g30: نه here works as an emphatic interjection 'nay/no' at the start of the next comment. زین تاختن is a verbal-nominal phrase built from the infinitive تاختن 'to charge/gallop' with زین 'saddle' specifying manner/instrument ('charging on the saddle'). بیژن is the agent/name, and آگاه نیست is the predicate 'is not unaware' (literally 'is not aware' if read plainly, but in context the rhetoric yields the sense 'he is not ignorant' / 'he is experienced'). Word order places the infinitival phrase before the subject and the predicative adjective after it, which is common in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s776-g31: همانگه 'at that moment' is the temporal adverb. به بیژن marks the indirect object 'to Bijan' (the person who receives the news). رسید is the past tense 'reached' (news reached someone). آگهی is 'news/information' — here the subject/thing that arrives to Bijan.
segments.s776-g32: که introduces the content of the news ('that ...'). آمد is the past 'came' (used here in a passive/result sense 'had come/come into'). بدست in the phrase بدست آمدن is idiomatically 'to fall into the hands of / to be taken by' (so the sense is the locative/possessive 'in the hand'). آن نهانی رهی is the noun phrase 'that hidden/secret wayfarer' (آن = that, نهانی = hidden/secret, رهی = wayfarer). Together the clause reports that the hidden wayfarer had come into [their] hands / had been captured.
segments.s776-g17: مر here functions as a dative/benefactive pronoun (an archaic form like مرا/مرا به), ‘to/for me’, introducing that the action concerns the speaker or narrator’s stake; او را is the patient with the accusative marker را (object is fronted before the verb for emphasis). بریگ فرب is a noun phrase (stout/robust girth or strap; two adjectives/nouns in apposition) that the verb دربیافت (compound past) governs; دربیافت is an older past form meaning ‘seized/caught/lay hold of’ (the verb appears at the end as typical SOV order).
segments.s776-g18: رکابش is a possessive noun (رکاب + ـش ‘his stirrup’) and گران کرد is a light-verb construction: گران ‘heavy’ + کرد ‘made/caused’ → ‘made (it) heavy / pulled it down’. The coordinating و links this clause to the next, and اندر شتافت uses اندر ‘into/within’ + شتافت (past of شتابیدن) meaning ‘hurried/rushed toward’; again verb at end in standard narrative order.
segments.s776-g19: چو here is the temporal subordinating conjunction ‘when’. نزدیک modifies the implied subject ‘he’ (‘having drawn near’) and ماهو برابر is an idiomatic phrase meaning ‘face to face / opposite him’ (ماهو = ‘that which is’ / ‘that one’, برابر = ‘opposite’); ببود is the archaic past copula ‘was’, placed after the predicate as is common in older Persian.
segments.s776-g20: نزد is a preposition meaning ‘near / in the presence of’; خنجر is the instrument ‘dagger’. او را with را marks the person affected (him). دلیری is an abstract noun ‘bravery/valor’, and نمود is the past of نمودن here meaning ‘showed / displayed’ (noun → verb: ‘displayed bravery’). Word order places the verb نمود at the end following its objects.
segments.s776-g21: کمربند بگرفت is a simple past action: کمربند ‘belt/girth’ as direct object and بگرفت (ب‑ prefix + گرفت) ‘he seized/took hold of’ — the ب‑ here is the historical past-prefix used in many classical Persian verbs. و links the next clause; او را ز زین uses the object marker را on او and the preposition ز (poetic form of از) meaning ‘from’ + زین ‘saddle’ to indicate the source ('from the saddle').
segments.s776-g22: برآورد here functions as a verbal compound meaning ‘he brought up / pulled up / extracted’ (a causative/resultative sense; classical texts use برآورد for ‘removed’ or ‘lifted off’), followed by و. آسان is an adverbial/qualifier (‘gently / lightly’), and بزد is the past of زدن ‘to strike’ (he struck; verb-final). بر زمین places the result on the ground (بر = onto, زمین = ground).
segments.s776-g23: فرود آمد is a compound past meaning ‘he alighted / came down’ (فرود = descent, آمد = came). The coordinating و links to the next action. دست او را with را marks the object (‘his hands’ / ‘the hands of him’) and ببست is past of بستن in the sense ‘he bound/tied’ (verb at the end as usual).
segments.s776-g24: به پیش اندر افگند is a sequence: به پیش اندر (lit. ‘toward before/in front’) frames the direction/placement and افگند (past of افکندن) means ‘he threw/cast (him)’, so together ‘he threw him forward/in front’. The conjunction و links the final clause; خود is the emphatic/reflexive pronoun ‘himself’, and برنشست is a past compound meaning ‘he sat upon / he took a seat’ (بر‑ + نشست), with the verb again final.
segments.s776-g81: بِزرگان is the plural subject 'the great ones/elders'. بر آن دوده نفرین کنند literally places the curse 'upon that dūdeh' — بر is the preposition 'upon/against', آن is the demonstrative 'that', دوده here is poetic/metaphorical (used for the kin/lineage in context) and does not match the literal senses given, and نفرین کنند is the present/plural form of کردن used to make 'to utter/make a curse' (they curse). The verb کنند is a narrative/habitual present here (third-person plural).
segments.s776-g82: سر از کشتن ... کنند uses the idiom سر از ... کردن 'to take to / to begin doing something' — سر is idiomatic here (not the literal 'head'), از is the preposition 'from/of', کشتن is the verbal noun 'killing', شاه پرکین is the direct object (the king named Perkin), and کنند is again the plural verb 'they do/undertake'. The whole clause means 'they took to killing King Perkin.'
segments.s776-g83: که introduces an optative/wish here ('that' → 'may it be that'). نفرین برو باد is an optative formula: نفرین (curse) + برو (go) + باد (optative particle 'may it be'), giving 'may the curse go forth'. و هرگز مباد adds 'and may it never be' — مباد is an unchanged optative-negative formula meaning 'may it not happen'.
segments.s776-g84: که marks a subordinate clause expressing the undesired outcome. او را is the third‑person singular object 'him' with the definite/object marker را. نه before نفرین negates (here 'not a/ no curse'); نفرین is 'curse' and فرستد is the subjunctive/optative third‑person singular of 'to send' ('that he may send'). بداد is a noun meaning 'injustice/oppression' and here functions as the thing sent/inflicted — 'send him a curse or inflict injustice.'
segments.s776-g85: کنون 'now' begins the temporal clause. زین سپس is a temporal phrase 'from this / after this' (زین here is a contracted / poetic form of 'از این' and does not match the literal sense list provided). دور عمر بود literally reads 'dūr ʿUmar būd' — in context عمر is a proper name (Umar), and دور here signals distance/continuance, so the clause means 'from then on Umar remained/continued (lived on).' بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s776-g86: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. دین آورد is 'religion came/brought' (دین 'religion'; آورد 'came/was brought'). تخت منبر بود is a contrastive image: تخت 'throne' + منبر 'pulpit' + بود 'was' — 'the throne served as a pulpit' (i.e., the royal seat became a place of religious preaching). The verb بودن is the simple past copula here.
segments.s776-g73: چو + X + باد is an archaic/poetic optative formula: چو means 'as/like' and باد is an optative particle here ('may he be'). آنکه begins a relative clause 'he who...', and بر جان شاه is a prepositional phrase ('against/on the king's life') that attaches to آنکه as its object. Word order places the simile and optative first, then the relative phrase describing the person wished upon.
segments.s776-g74: نبخشود is a classical past negative form meaning 'did not spare/forgive' (describing the habitual/past action of the person). هرگز 'never' negates the possibility; مبیناد here is an archaic present/impersonal verb 'appear / be seen' and گاه 'time' completes the idiom 'نِه گاه دیدن' — 'to see a time/occasion'. Together the clause says that such a man never sees a (favorable) time.
segments.s776-g75: The numeral + noun phrase 'سه پور جوانش' = 'his three young sons' — پور 'son' with the possessive suffix -ش on جوان ('his young ones'). به + لشکر means 'to/into the army'. بدند here is a past plural verbal form meaning 'they were placed / they went' (classical usage for 'were sent/assigned').
segments.s776-g76: همان هر سه emphatically repeats 'each/ all three of them'. با is the preposition 'with' and links the instruments/attire 'تخت و افسر' (تخت here 'throne', افسر an older word for a helmet or crown). بدند again is past plural 'they were' (i.e., were invested/received). The clause says each of the three received throne-and-helmet (royal accoutrements).
segments.s776-g77: همان جایگه 'that same place' (همان intensifies جایگه 'place/position'). آتشی بر فروخت is a poetic order: آتشی برفروخت = 'he kindled/raised a fire upon it' — فروخت here is the older/poetic verb 'to kindle', a past tense indicating the action performed at that place.
segments.s776-g78: پدر را and هر سه پسر را use the object marker را to mark definite/affected objects. بسوخت is the past (causative) form meaning '(he) burned (them) up' — active past tense showing the agent burned the father and the three sons.
segments.s776-g79: از آن تخمه 'from that lineage/stock' sets the domain; کس نماند 'no one remained' uses کس in the negative sense 'no one' and نماند (past) 'remained/was left'. در زمانه 'in that time/age' gives the temporal frame. The clause states that none of that family survived.
segments.s776-g80: وگر = 'and if / but if'. ماند is the present/past stem 'remained'. هرکو is an older fused form meaning 'whoever/anyone who'. بدیدش is the verb بدید 'saw' with the object suffix -ش 'him'. براند (third-person singular) means 'drove away'. Together: if anyone had remained, whoever saw him would drive him away.
segments.s776-g65: بفرمود is an honorific past-tense verb 'he ordered/said' (used here to report a royal command). تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that / in order that'). گوش و بینیش is the direct object: گوش 'ear' plus بینیش = بین + -ش, a possessive enclitic (-ش) meaning 'his nose/sight' (here paired with 'ear' — the exact classical sense of بینیش is rare but context shows it names a facial organ). پست is an adjective 'low'/'down' modifying the following verb phrase implicitly (see next segment).
segments.s776-g66: بریدند is 3rd person plural past 'they cut' (here a passive effect on the victim but grammatically an active past). و simply connects the two clauses. خود is emphatic 'he himself' (subject-focus). بارگی is an uncommon/uncertain noun here (literally 'leaf/foliage' in one sense); in context it names what he sat upon. برنشست is a compound verb: بر- + نشست (past) 'sat upon' — the prefix بر- expresses 'on/upon'.
segments.s776-g67: بفرمود again functions as 'he ordered'. کاین is an archaic demonstrative 'that/this' (k + این); را is the definite direct-object marker attaching to کاین (so 'that (one)' as the object). بر + ین here form برین (poetic/archaic) giving a locative sense 'on that'; ریگ گرم = 'hot sand' (ریگ 'sand', گرم 'hot'). The phrase as a whole marks where/to what he gave the order.
segments.s776-g68: بدارید here is an imperative/ hortative plural 'keep/put (it)' (classical command form). تا introduces the final-purpose clause 'until/so that ...'. خوابش = خواب + -ش 'his sleep' (i.e. 'sleep comes to him'). آید is the present/subjunctive 'may come' or 'comes', used after تا to express the desired result. ز is the poetic form of از 'from/because of', and شرم means 'shame' — 'until sleep comes to him from shame.'
segments.s776-g69: منادیگری is a verbal noun 'heralding / the act of proclaiming' and serves as the subject/topic. گرد لشکر is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'around the host/army' (گرد = around/about, لشکر = army). بگشت is an archaic past/past-tense verbal form 'went/turned' (here 'went about' — the b- prefix is classical).
segments.s776-g70: به is the preposition 'to/toward'. درگاه here means 'threshold/entrance' (of a tent) rather than a royal court. هر خیمه‌ای 'each tent' uses هر 'every' with خیمه + the enclitic -ای marking indefinite/each. برگذشت is a past verb 'passed by' (بر- + گذشت 'went/passed'). The word order is prepositional phrase then verb: 'he passed by to the doorway of each tent.'
segments.s776-g71: که‌ای is a vocative/exclamatory address 'O ...' (classical/poetic 'که ای' used to call attention). بندگان is vocative plural 'servants' or 'subjects'. خداوندکش is an epithet/apposition 'king-slayer' (lit. 'one who kills the lord'); here it qualifies or identifies the person they serve or fear. The three words form a vocative phrase addressing the camp.
segments.s776-g72: مشورید is an imperative/hortative plural 'consult / hold counsel' (addressing the listeners). بیهوده is an adverb 'in vain/pointless' (here a warning not to act uselessly). هر جای 'in every place' (هر 'every' + جای 'place'), and هش is an interjection 'beware!' or 'take heed' — together: 'Consult — do not (act) in vain; everywhere, beware!'
segments.s776-g49: چنین داد پاسخ is a literary word order: adverb چنين (‘thus’) + past verb داد (‘gave’) + object پاسخ (‘answer’). که introduces the content clause (‘that …’). از بدکنش is a prepositional phrase: از ‘from/out of’ + بدکنش (‘bad action/ill conduct’, here with the enclitic -ش = ‘his/its’), i.e. ‘from his ill-doing’.
segments.s776-g50: نیاید is a literary present/optative form meaning ‘does not come / will not arise’ (here: ‘nothing comes’). مگر is the restrictive particle ‘except’. کشتن and سرزنش are nouns joined by و, giving the exception: ‘except killing and reproach.’
segments.s776-g51: بدین بد is an idiomatic phrase meaning ‘with this evil / thus evil’ (با + این / ‘by this’ + بد ‘evil’). کنون is the adverb ‘now’. گردن من (noun + pronoun) ‘my neck’ appears before the imperative verb بزن (‘strike’/‘cut’); Persian commonly places object(s) before the verb. بزن is an imperative (second-person) commanding the action.
segments.s776-g52: بینداز is an imperative ‘throw/cast (it)’. در پیش is a prepositional idiom meaning ‘before / in the presence of’ and is followed by this انجمن ‘this assembly/meeting’ as its object. Word order: verb (imperative) + locative phrase ‘در پیش این انجمن’.
segments.s776-g53: بترسید is an exhortation/imperative ‘be afraid’ or ‘beware’. The clause that follows has an implied conjunction: کش here functions as an agentive element (‘the puller/executioner’ or ‘the killer’) and پوست بیرون کشد is the verb-final clause: پوست (object ‘skin’) + بیرون (adverb ‘out’) + کشد (3rd-person verb ‘will pull’). Classical Persian places the verb at the end.
segments.s776-g54: تنش را is the definite object marker را attached to تنش (‘his body’ = تن + -ش). بدان کینه is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘with that grudge / with that hatred’ (بدان = با + آن, ‘with that’ + کینه ‘grudge’). در خون is a locative ‘in/through blood’. کشد is the verb (‘will drag/drag along’); syntactically the object (تنش را) precedes the verb as usual in Persian.
segments.s776-g55: نهانش is ‘hidden/secret’ with the enclitic -ش (‘it/his’) and functions here as the thing perceived or as an adverbial nuance ‘secretly’. بدانست is a past tense verb ‘knew it / perceived it’ (بد + دانست in classical spelling). مرد دلیر is the subject ‘the brave man’ (noun + adjective). Word order here is subject after the verb phrase (poetic flexibility) but the sense is ‘the brave man knew/perceived it, secretly’.
segments.s776-g56: به پاسخ = ‘to/for an answer’ (indicating relation to replying). زمانی ‘for a while’ (temporal adverb). همی‌بود is a literary imperfect/continuous past ‘he was / he kept being’ and here indicates continued delay. دیر is a predicate adverb/adjective ‘late / long (in time)’ — together the clause means ‘he delayed for some time before replying’.
segments.s776-g57: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase meaning “thus he answered” (چنین = thus; داد پاسخ = gave an answer). The conjunction که introduces the content of his reply. ایدون کنم is first‑person with کنم (present/subjunctive) expressing what he should do; ایدون is an adverbial/interrogative element (poetic/archaic) indicating ‘now/what now’ or ‘what shall I do’. The clause is reported speech introduced by که.
segments.s776-g58: که begins the subordinate content clause. کین is the object meaning ‘hatred/vengeance’. از دل خویش is a prepositional phrase ‘from/from out of (از) my heart’ where دل = heart and خوی + ش expresses a reflexive/possessive “one’s own / his own” (here ‘my/his own’). بیرون کنم uses بیرون (out) plus کنم (subjunctive/infinitival sense of ‘to make/drive out’) to express the desire/purpose of removing that hatred from the heart.
segments.s776-g59: بدین (here written in two parts in the word list) functions as a demonstrative/prepositional modifier meaning ‘by/with these’ or ‘because of these’. It introduces a coordinated list of abstract qualities: مردی (manliness), دانش (knowledge), رای (judgment/counsel), and خوی (disposition/temperament). The conjunction و links the items in the usual Persian coordinate order.
segments.s776-g60: همی is a poetic/emphatic particle (here emphatic) preceding the predicate. تاج و تخت are the coordinated nouns ‘crown and throne’. آمدت is a past/resultive sense ‘it came to you / it occurred to you’ (with enclitic ـت marking the second person indirect object), and آرزوی is the noun ‘desire/wish’ placed after the verb; together the phrase means ‘the crown and throne became your desire’ (i.e. you desired the crown and throne). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s776-g61: به شمشیر is an instrumental prepositional phrase ‘with/by the sword’. دستش is the possessed noun ‘his hand’ (دست + enclitic ش). ببرید here records the cutting action; in this poetic narrative it functions as a past/perfective form ‘they cut’ (or as an order, depending on context). و گفت links to the subsequent speech ‘and said’. Note that no را is used before دستش because possession is expressed by the enclitic.
segments.s776-g62: که introduces the content of what was said. این دست را uses را as the definite object marker (‘this hand’ as the object). در بدی literally ‘in evil/wickedness’ sets the domain for the negation. نیست is the negation ‘is not/there is not’. جفت means ‘pair/equal’ — together the clause says ‘there is no equal/pair to this hand in wickedness’, i.e. ‘this hand has no match in doing evil.’
segments.s776-g63: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning ‘when’. دستش ببرید is the past event ‘his hand was cut’ (دست + enclitic ش + verb). گفتا is the reporting verb ‘he said’ (poetic past). دو پا here is the object of the command that follows (‘two legs’ = both legs). The sequence sets up reported speech after the cutting.
segments.s776-g64: ببرید is the imperative/command form ‘cut (them) off’ addressed to agents (here ‘cut off [his legs]’). تا introduces a purpose/result clause meaning ‘so that’ or ‘in order that’. ماند is the verb ‘remain’ (substantive/result) and together with ایدر (ایدر = here) بجا (‘in place’/‘in that place’) expresses the intended result ‘so that he remains here / remains in place.’

sentences.s776-s5: One should not let the Syrian Māhūy be afraid of battle; take him to the Jihun at once.
sentences.s776-s6: Do not take your eyes off him in haste, for with him we shall alter the affair.
sentences.s776-s7: When Barsam saw the Chinese banner, he drew the army aside to one flank.
sentences.s776-s8: He kept charging until before the sandy bank of Farb, his ornate face full and his lips full of curses.
sentences.s776-s17: A world-seeking, spiritual, distraught one — full of cruelty, godless, and a slayer of lords.
sentences.s776-s18: When Bijan heard that he became joyful, swelled with pride, and was freed from care.
sentences.s776-s19: They struck (or made) شراعی upon the soft sand; the ماهوی went like a warm wind.
sentences.s776-s20: When the sinner saw Bijan’s face, his reason was broken and the brain in his head vanished.
sentences.s776-s1: When Bijan marshaled the whole army into line, he resolved to lie in ambush for the Iranians.
sentences.s776-s2: He perceived the reality/essence, and from the heart he burst forth tumultuously out of the midst of the army.
sentences.s776-s3: Bijan kept watch and saw his banner; he knew where he would leap.
sentences.s776-s4: He told Barsam: 'From the center, move the army you have to one side.'
sentences.s776-s21: Out of fear he became like a lifeless body; flowing sand was scattered upon his head.
sentences.s776-s22: Bijan said to him, 'O low‑born one! May it never be that someone like you be a guardian of anyone.'
sentences.s776-s23: Why did you kill that just king? May God grant victory and long life.
sentences.s776-s24: A father to the king's father and to the king himself — from the sweet‑souled a memorial in the world.
sentences.s776-s13: At that moment his companions arrived, and the whole plain became full of talk about him.
sentences.s776-s14: They told Babr-sam, 'Do not take this one; the axe must strike his neck.'
sentences.s776-s15: He answered thus: 'This is not the way — no, Bijan is not unaware of charging from the saddle.'
sentences.s776-s16: At that moment news reached Bijan that the hidden wayfarer had been taken (had fallen into their hands).
sentences.s776-s9: He seized him by the stout girth, pulled down on his stirrup and rushed in.
sentences.s776-s10: When he had drawn near and stood opposite him, he displayed his bravery with a dagger drawn near.
sentences.s776-s11: He grabbed the belt, pulled him from the saddle, and lightly struck him to the ground.
sentences.s776-s12: He dismounted, bound his hands, threw him forward before them, and himself sat down.
sentences.s776-s41: The elders cursed that lineage and set about killing King Perkin.
sentences.s776-s42: May that curse go forth, and may it never be that he sends him a curse or does him injustice.
sentences.s776-s43: From that time onward Umar lived on; when religion arrived, the throne became a pulpit.
sentences.s776-s37: May he be like the moon — he who never spared the king's life shall never see a time (of pardon).
sentences.s776-s38: He put his three young sons into the army; each of the three was equipped with a throne and a helmet.
sentences.s776-s39: At that very place he kindled a fire, and he burned the father and all three sons.
sentences.s776-s40: Of that stock no one remained in that age; and if anyone did remain, whoever saw him drove him away.
sentences.s776-s33: He ordered that their ears and nose be cut off, and he himself sat upon a bar(g)i.
sentences.s776-s34: He ordered that they keep him on that hot sand until sleep comes upon him from shame.
sentences.s776-s35: A herald went about the army and passed by the entrance of each tent.
sentences.s776-s36: “O servants of the king-slayer, consult — do not be idle — in every place, beware!”
sentences.s776-s25: He answered thus that from his ill conduct nothing would come except killing and reproach.
sentences.s776-s26: Do this evil now: strike my neck and cast it before this assembly.
sentences.s776-s27: Be afraid — the executioner will pull off the skin, and with that grudge will drag his body in blood.
sentences.s776-s28: The brave man perceived it secretly; he delayed for a while before answering.
sentences.s776-s29: He answered thus: "What shall I do now so that I may drive this hatred out of my heart?"
sentences.s776-s30: By these qualities — manliness, learning, counsel, and temperament — the crown and throne became your desire.
sentences.s776-s31: They cut off his hand with the sword, and said, "There is no equal to this hand in wickedness."
sentences.s776-s32: When his hand was cut off, he said, "Cut off his two legs as well, so that he remain here in place."
